resources.string: ‚Ä¢ Power with Natural Exponent: a‚Åø means multiplying a by itself n times; a¬π = a. a is the base, n is the exponent.\n ‚Ä¢ Common names: a¬≤ (square), a¬≥ (cube).\n ‚Ä¢ Root (Evolution): the inverse of powers. The nth root ‚Åø‚àöa gives a number whose nth power equals a. ‚àöa is the square root, ¬≥‚àöa the cube root.

‚Ä¢ Even powers of negatives are positive; odd powers stay negative.
 ‚Ä¢ 0‚Åø = 0, 1‚Åø = 1.
 ‚Ä¢ Some roots (like ‚àö2) are not rational.
 ‚Ä¢ Approximate roots satisfy b‚Åø < a < (b + Œ±)‚Åø.

‚Ä¢ Irrational numbers have nonterminating, nonrepeating decimals (e.g., ‚àö2 = 1.414... ).
 ‚Ä¢ Every positive irrational has a negative counterpart.
 ‚Ä¢ Equal irrationals share identical decimal digits.
 ‚Ä¢ Examples: ‚àö2, œÄ, and most logarithms or trigonometric values.

‚Ä¢ Irrationals come from roots of non-perfect powers.
 ‚Ä¢ They can‚Äôt be written as fractions.
 ‚Ä¢ Comparison is done digit by digit.
 ‚Ä¢ œÄ and ‚àö3 are classic examples.

‚Ä¢ Real Numbers: include all rationals and irrationals.
 ‚Ä¢ Arithmetic laws (commutative, associative, distributive) apply to them.
 ‚Ä¢ Irrationals + rationals may yield rational or irrational results.
 ‚Ä¢ Arithmetical nth root: for a ‚â• 0, there‚Äôs one non-negative b such that b‚Åø = a.
 ‚Ä¢ Coordinates: any point (x, y) lies in the plane; distance |M‚ÇÅM‚ÇÇ| = ‚àö((x‚ÇÇ‚àíx‚ÇÅ)¬≤ + (y‚ÇÇ‚àíy‚ÇÅ)¬≤).

‚Ä¢ Power Rules:
 1. a·µê¬∑a‚Åø = a·µê‚Å∫‚Åø
 2. a·µê / a‚Åø = a·µê‚Åª‚Åø
 3. (a·µê)‚Åø = a·µê‚Åø
 4. (ab)‚Åø = a‚Åøb‚Åø, (a/b)‚Åø = a‚Åø/b‚Åø
 5. (-a)‚Åø = a‚Åø if n even, -a‚Åø if odd.
‚Ä¢ Square root: ‚àöa = b if b¬≤ = a (a ‚â• 0).\n ‚Ä¢ Only one non-negative square root exists.\n ‚Ä¢ Key relation: ‚àöa¬≤ = |a|.
‚Ä¢ ‚àöab = ‚àöa¬∑‚àöb, ‚àö(a/b) = ‚àöa/‚àöb.\n ‚Ä¢ If a > b > 0, then ‚àöa > ‚àöb.\n ‚Ä¢ Simplify roots: ‚àöa¬≤b = |a|‚àöb; a‚àöb = ‚àöa¬≤b.
‚Ä¢ Zero exponent: a‚Å∞ = 1 (for a ‚âÝ 0).\n ‚Ä¢ Negative exponent: a‚Åª‚Åø = 1/a‚Åø.\n ‚Ä¢ 0‚Å∞ and 0‚Åª‚Åø are undefined.

‚Ä¢ Power laws for integer exponents:
 1. a·µñ¬∑a·µÝ = a·µñ‚Å∫·µÝ
 2. (a·µñ)·µÝ = a·µñ·µÝ
 3. a·µñ/a·µÝ = a·µñ‚Åª·µÝ
 4. (ab)·µñ = a·µñb·µñ, (a/b)·µñ = a·µñ/b·µñ.

‚Ä¢ Arithmetical nth root: ‚Åø‚àöa = b if b‚Åø = a (a ‚â• 0).
 ‚Ä¢ Rules:
 1. ‚Åø‚àöa = ‚Åø·µè‚àöa·µè
 2. ‚Åø‚àöa¬∑‚Åø‚àöb = ‚Åø‚àö(ab)
 3. ‚Åø‚àöa/‚Åø‚àöb = ‚Åø‚àö(a/b)
 4. (‚Åø‚àöa)·µê = ‚Åø‚àöa·µê
 5. ·µê‚àö‚Åø‚àöa = ·µê‚Åø‚àöa.
 ‚Ä¢ Like roots: simplifiable only when indices and radicands match.
 ‚Ä¢ Key identity: ‚Åø‚àöa‚Åø = |a| if n even; = a if n odd.
‚Ä¢ For a > 0 and x = p/q, aÀ£ = a^(p/q) = ‚Åπ‚àöa·µñ.\n ‚Ä¢ If a = 0 and x > 0, then aÀ£ = 0.\n ‚Ä¢ Extends powers to rational exponents.
‚Ä¢ Power rules remain valid for rational exponents and positive bases.
‚Ä¢ For irrational exponents, aÀ£ is defined by limits of rational approximations.\n ‚Ä¢ a‚ÅªÀ£ = 1/aÀ£.\n ‚Ä¢ The process gives a unique positive result for a > 0.

‚Ä¢ Power laws for real exponents:
 1. aÀ£¬∑a ∏ = aÀ£‚Å∫ ∏
 2. (aÀ£) ∏ = aÀ£ ∏
 3. aÀ£/a ∏ = aÀ£‚Åª ∏
 4. (ab)À£ = aÀ£bÀ£
 5. (a/b)À£ = aÀ£/bÀ£.
